loadError.title: Could not load dashboard data
loadError.bodyWithMessage: {message}. The site usually recovers on the next daily refresh.
loadError.bodyDefault: The metrics file is unreachable. The site usually recovers on the next daily refresh.

overview.sectionLede: An ASmap maps an ASN to IP networks under its control. These maps are generated on a regular basis. These cards summarise the build you pick, measure how much of the internet it maps, and gauge how much it has changed since the last release.
overview.noPublishedMaps: No published maps found in metrics.json.
overview.noVariantData: Build {name} has no published variant data.

buildSelector.ariaLabel: ASmap build for Overview metrics. Newest builds listed first.

fallbackBadge.label: Filled fallback
fallbackBadge.tooltip: This build did not publish an unfilled variant. Numbers are read from the filled (embedded) file instead.

entries.label: Entries
entries.unit: IP ranges mapped to an operator
info: Each entry maps one IP prefix to the autonomous system that announces it, so this is the map's raw coverage.
Read from the unfilled (source data) variant, falling back to filled when a build published only that.

The vs previous delta shows only when both builds share an encoding. Filling folds same-AS neighbours and cuts the count by ~12 percent, so a mixed comparison would fake a jump. The on-disk size rides along in the entries chart tooltip.

uniqueAses.label: Unique ASes
info: How many distinct autonomous systems the map references. Each AS is one peer diversity bucket Bitcoin Core can keep a connection to.
Independent of the variant: filling adds prefixes, never new ASes.
The bar below splits the mapping entries by IPv4 versus IPv6 prefixes.

drift.label: Drift vs previous
drift.filledOnlyHint: Source data drift needs an unfilled variant.
info: Share of the mapped IPv4 address space that lands on a different autonomous system between this build and the most recent one it can be diffed against. At 1 percent, about 1 mapped IPv4 address in 100 moved. Mapped means at least one of the two builds assigned it.

IPv4 leads because Bitcoin Core peer reachability leans on it most. IPv6 rides along as a second line: the two families cannot be added, since IPv6 is larger by 2 to the power of 96 and would round IPv4 down to noise.
Computed unfilled vs unfilled, so this is real source data drift, not fill heuristic shuffling.

history.sectionLede: How every published build changed over time, from how much address space moved to a different operator to how fast the map's coverage grew. Use the range and family pickers above to focus on a window or on one address family.
history.emptyDrift: Need at least two builds and one diff to plot drift.
history.allSeriesHidden: All series hidden. Click a legend entry to bring one back.
history.noDriftData: No drift data for the picked mode.

driftUnit.ariaLabel: Address family for drift (IPv4 or IPv6)

driftCumulative.title: Cumulative Drift Since the Oldest Build
driftCumulative.lede: An ASmap maps an IP network to the autonomous system, or AS, that announces it. This chart adds up how far each build has moved from the oldest one, meaning the share of mapped address space that now points to a different AS. A flat stretch means the data held steady, while a steep climb means a large change.
driftCumulative.ariaLabel: Cumulative drift composition since the oldest published build. Reassigned, newly mapped, unmapped, and total drift series.
info: How far each build has drifted from the oldest build with an unfilled variant. The lines only grow, so a flat stretch is a quiet baseline and a steep slope is fast change.

info.text: Prefix mapped in both builds but now on a different autonomous system. Usually the largest slice; real BGP and RPKI churn.
Prefix had no autonomous system in the baseline and now resolves to one. Coverage growing as upstream adds prefixes.
Prefix that resolved in the baseline no longer does. Upstream dropped it, usually pulled-back RPKI or IRR coverage.
The three summed: the headline distance from the baseline for the selected build.

info: The IPv4/IPv6 picker at the top of History switches every chart between families. They stay apart because Bitcoin Core treats them as independent and a combined denominator would round IPv4 down to noise.
Click a legend entry to hide that line and rescale. Builds without an unfilled variant carry no dot; the line bridges across them.

driftStep.title: Drift Between Builds
driftStep.lede: The same change measured from one build to the next, with one bar for how much moved between each release and the one before it. Tall bars are big updates and short bars are small ones. A tall bar right after a long pause is mostly the months in between, not a single event.
driftStep.ariaLabel: Step drift composition between each build and its last diffable predecessor. Reassigned, newly mapped, unmapped, and total drift series.
info: How much each build changed against the previous build with an unfilled variant. One stacked bar per release: tall moved a lot, flat is quiet.

info.text: Prefix mapped in both builds but now on a different autonomous system.
Prefix that was unmapped in the previous build now resolves to an autonomous system.
Prefix that resolved in the previous build no longer does.
The full stack height. In IPv4 mode it matches the Drift vs previous card for the same pair.

info: Bars are not time-normalised. A build after a five month publishing pause stacks up five months of churn in one bar, so a tall bar after a long gap is mostly elapsed time, not one event. Each bar's tooltip names the gap.
The IPv4/IPv6 picker switches this chart between families; click a legend entry to hide one. Builds without an unfilled variant have no comparable predecessor and show as an empty column.

entriesChart.title: Source Data Entries Over Time
entriesChart.lede: A build is really a list of entries, each tying one block of IP addresses to its operator. This tracks how that list grows over time, covering both the raw source data and the smaller compressed version Bitcoin Core actually ships. A rising line means the map covers more of the internet.
entriesChart.infoAria: About the entries over time chart
entriesChart.ariaLabel: ASmap entries count over time, embedded vs source data variant. Hover the chart for exact values per build.
entriesChart.noPublishedVariants: No published variants for the loaded builds.
info: How many prefix to AS entries each build carries, tracking coverage growth.

info.text: Raw upstream prefixes the build was made from. Grows as upstream advertises more.
After the fill heuristic folds same-AS neighbours into single rows. Always lower; the gap to unfilled is the compression.

info: Hover a build for both counts, the on-disk size, and the compression ratio. Builds missing a variant carry no dot, but the line bridges across them.

mapDeltaChart.title: Source Data Entries Delta Between Builds
mapDeltaChart.lede: The change in that entry count from one build to the next, meaning how many address blocks each build added or dropped compared with the one before. Bars above the line added coverage. Bars below it lost coverage, usually when an operator's routing data was withdrawn.
mapDeltaChart.ariaLabel: Source data entry count delta between each ASmap build and its last diffable predecessor. Hover each bar for details.
mapDeltaChart.empty: Need at least two builds with an unfilled variant to plot deltas.
info: Gain or loss in source data prefix entries between each build and the most recent earlier build with an unfilled variant.
A positive bar added that many real prefix to ASN entries; a negative bar lost them, usually pulled-back RPKI or IRR coverage.

Computed unfilled vs unfilled. A build whose immediate predecessor is filled only falls back to the last unfilled build, so one filled-only release no longer blanks the surrounding bars. Hover any bar for the exact counts and the date diffed against.

diversityChart.title: Distinct Operators Per Build
diversityChart.lede: Count of unique AS in each ASmap. The drift charts above measured how much the map moved; this counts how many operators it can see.
diversityChart.ariaLabel: Count of distinct network operators (autonomous systems) mapped per ASmap build. More operators means a node is harder to surround.
diversityChart.empty: Need at least two builds to plot operator counts.
info: How many distinct operators each build maps to at least one block of addresses. Every operator is one network Bitcoin Core can hold a separate connection to under the asmap GetGroup() rule.

Coverage drift asks how much address space moved; this asks how many distinct operators exist. Reassigning address blocks between operators already in the map leaves it flat; one block on a brand-new operator lifts it by one.
Computed from the unfilled variant, so the curve reflects real upstream routing, not the fill heuristic. Filled-only builds carry no dot and are bridged across.

network.noData: No data for the visible series.
network.allSeriesHidden: All series hidden. Click a legend entry to bring one back.

overview.sectionLede: This tab tests each ASmap build against the real Bitcoin nodes that crawlers observe on the network, so you can see how well a build actually groups the peers a node meets in practice.
overview.empty: No observed node snapshots in network.json.
overview.clearnetNote: The cards count only clearnet (IPv4/IPv6) reachable nodes — Tor, I2P, and CJDNS peers have no IP for ASmap to resolve to an autonomous system, so they are left out.
overview.snapshotMeta: {source} crawl, latest snapshot {date} - scored against ASmap build {build}

nodes.label: Reachable nodes
info: Distinct clearnet IPs the crawler reached in its latest snapshot. Onion, I2P, and CJDNS peers are left out: they have no IP for ASmap to resolve to an autonomous system.
The split counts each peer by effective address family, so an IPv4-in-IPv6 tunnel (6to4, NAT64) counts as IPv4, exactly as Bitcoin Core's GetGroup() does.

concentration.label: AS concentration
info: Pick two observed nodes at random: this number is the chance they sit in the same autonomous system. At 0.02 that chance is 2 percent.

It is the Herfindahl-Hirschman index: each AS's share of the mapped nodes squared, then summed. Squaring is what makes it a concentration measure, so a few large operators dominate it and the long tail barely registers.
It runs from near 0 (spread across many operators) to 1 (every node in one AS); lower is healthier. As a rule of thumb, 1 divided by the HHI is the effective number of equal sized operators.
HHI leads instead of the largest operator's share, which can fall while concentration just shifts to the runner-up. The split line below repeats it per family.

reach50.label: ASes to reach 50%
reach50.unit: operators that together hold half the mapped nodes
reach50.noData: no mapped nodes in this snapshot
info: Imagine an attacker buying up whole network operators one by one, biggest first. This is how many they would need before half of all mapped nodes sit inside operators they control. Higher is healthier: it means no small handful of operators is close to holding half the network.

Sort the operators by how many nodes they host, then add them up from the top until the running total passes half. The number of operators that took is the figure shown. Nodes the map cannot place are left out, exactly as they are in AS concentration.

The 50% line is the standard cut-off decentralisation studies use, so the count stays comparable across projects. It is a headcount of operators, not a claim that those operators actually control the network.
reach50.infoAria: About how many ASes it takes to reach 50% of nodes

buckets.label: Peer diversity buckets
buckets.unit: operator buckets the nodes fall into
info: Bitcoin Core sorts every peer into a bucket and refuses to fill all its connection slots from one bucket, so an attacker can't surround a node by owning a single corner of the internet. With ASmap loaded a bucket is a whole network operator. This counts how many distinct buckets today's nodes spread across.

Without ASmap, Core buckets purely by neighbouring IP ranges (/16 for IPv4, /32 for IPv6), which splits one operator across many buckets. ASmap folds those back into one bucket per operator, so the count drops. That is the security gain: an attacker now needs whole operators, not just adjacent IP ranges, to dominate a peer's buckets. The line below shows how much fewer buckets that is.

staleness.label: Map staleness
staleness.unit: of today's nodes shift AS per year
staleness.basis: scaled from {drift} at {days}d of map age
staleness.basisInterpolated: {driftLower} at {daysLower}d and {driftUpper} at {daysUpper}d of map age
info: The decay curve below boiled down to one yearly rate: the share of today's nodes a one-year-old embedded map would place in the wrong autonomous system.

Read off the curve at one year of map age, interpolated between the two nearest builds (rarely is one exactly 365 days old). Only when the history is shorter than a year is the nearest build scaled instead. The line below names the readings used.
It answers how often Bitcoin Core should ship a fresh asmap: a low rate ages gracefully, a high one argues for refreshing more often.

latestUpdate.label: Latest update impact
info: How many currently observed nodes resolve to a different autonomous system after the most recent release, versus the build published just before it. It turns the map diff into a concrete count of peers moved.
Each node is looked up in both builds and bucketed like the Diff Explorer's prefixes: reassigned, newly mapped, or unmapped. The headline is the total moved; the line below splits it.
The node set is the latest crawl, so the figure ages with the data, not the visitor. A tiny count is routing noise; a big jump is a substantive re-mapping worth a look in the Diff Explorer.

charts.sectionLede: These trends follow how the map performs against the observed network over time, with each independent crawler drawn as its own line so they confirm one another. Click a legend entry to isolate one.

decay.title: Map staleness for today's nodes
decay.ledeTruth: How wrong an aging map is for the nodes a crawler sees today, checked against reality (the crawler's own whois): the share it files under a different operator than the node really sits in. Time runs from today on the left into the past on the right, so each line starts at its crawler's freshest snapshot and climbs as the map ages. Even the newest point sits above zero — the attribution gap against live routing — so where a line crosses your tolerance is how often a fresh map is worth shipping. Only crawlers that ship their own whois carry that ground truth, so bitnod.es is greyed out here; switch to “Newest map” to compare it too.
decay.ledeMap: How much an aging map reshuffles the nodes a crawler sees today versus that crawler's own freshest map: the share it moves to a different operator. Time runs from today on the left into the past on the right, and each line's freshest map is its own zero, so it starts at zero and climbs with age — isolating how much aging alone changes the buckets. Switch to “Reality” to measure against live routing instead.
decay.ariaLabel: Share of each crawler's most recent observed nodes that an older build resolves to a different autonomous system, against the picked reference. The axis runs from today on the left into the past on the right. One line per crawler.
decay.noWhois: No whois on this crawler's snapshots, so there is no ground truth to measure against here.
info: Each crawler's most recent node set, held fixed and looked up in every build at or older than that snapshot, plotted from today on the left into the past on the right. Holding the node set fixed means map age is the only thing changing between points.

Reality: each build's disagreement with the crawler's own whois ASN. The newest point equals the attribution gap (100 minus the agreement shown below) and age only adds to it — the direct “when do I need a fresher map?” reading. Each crawler is anchored on its newest whois-bearing snapshot, so a frozen archive starts at its last annotated crawl; crawlers without whois are greyed out here.
Newest map: each build versus that crawler's own freshest map, which sits at zero. This isolates how much aging alone reshuffles the buckets and works for every crawler.
One line per crawler. Where they overlap, the map ages the same no matter who observed the network.

hhi.title: AS concentration (HHI) over time
hhi.lede: The same concentration boiled down to a single score, the chance that two random nodes belong to the same operator. Lower is better, since it means nodes are spread across many operators rather than bunched into a few.
hhi.ariaLabel: Herfindahl-Hirschman index of the observed node set across every snapshot, scored against the build in effect at each one. One line per crawler. Lower means more diverse.
info: Pick two observed nodes at random: this index is the chance they sit in the same autonomous system. At 0.02 that chance is 2 percent.
The same HHI as the concentration card, but scored against the build in effect at each snapshot. A falling line means the network keeps spreading across more operators.
One line per crawler; where they agree, the trend holds no matter who observed the network.

The family switch scores IPv4 only or IPv6 only (tunneled IPv4-in-IPv6 counts as IPv4). All pools both, counting an operator's IPv4 and IPv6 nodes as one AS. IPv4 sets the overall level as the majority, but IPv6 runs far more concentrated, which All alone hides, so the split is worth watching.

coverage.title: ASmap coverage of observed nodes
coverage.lede: How much of the real network the map can place at all, meaning the share of observed nodes whose address it can match to an operator. A falling line means ASmap's data is lagging behind where new nodes are appearing.
coverage.ariaLabel: Share of each snapshot's clearnet nodes the build in effect resolves to an autonomous system. One line per crawler. Higher means the map fits the network better.
info: For each snapshot, the share of clearnet nodes the build in effect resolves to an autonomous system. The rest fall outside every prefix the map carries and use Bitcoin Core's default /16 and /32 groups.
The HHI chart asks how mapped nodes distribute; this asks how many get mapped at all. A falling line means the routing data behind ASmap is lagging where the nodes are.
Each snapshot is scored against the build in effect at its crawl time, so it reflects what a then current map actually delivered.

The dips are map staleness, not bad data: coverage sags as a map ages and snaps back at each release. The deepest (KIT, August 2024) was crawled hours before a release against a five-month-old build, so every node it missed resolves under the next one.

concentration.operatorsLede: Where those observed nodes actually sit, shown as the combined share of the network held by the five largest operators at each point in time. A shrinking stack means nodes are spreading across more operators, which is healthier, since no single operator comes close to surrounding a node.
concentration.operatorsAria: Stacked bars, one per snapshot of the selected crawler, segmented into that snapshot's five largest operators by node share. The bar height is the combined top-5 share. Hover a bar for the operator names.
operatorsInfo: One bar per snapshot, split into the five operators with the largest node share at that point, scored against the build in effect. Bar height is the combined top-5 share, so a shrinking bar means the top tier is losing its grip.

The top five are re-picked per snapshot, since a fixed five would understate the share when a new operator rises. Each operator keeps its colour across bars, so a colour appearing or vanishing is the tier reshuffling. Hover a bar for the names.
KIT only, the live crawl. The cross-crawler check lives in the decay and HHI charts above.

crosscheck.label: ASN attribution agreement
crosscheck.note: Share of nodes where the {source} crawl's own ASN tag matches the ASmap lookup for the same IP — a data-quality cross-check, not a network-health metric. A few percent always differ where whois and routing disagree, so a steady reading (both crawlers stay within a {min} to {max} band) is evidence the data behind every chart is sound.

diff.sectionLede: Pick any two builds to see exactly what changed between them, from the address blocks that switched operator to how many real observed nodes each edit moves.
diff.needTwoUnfilled: Need at least two builds with an unfilled variant to compare.

results.samePair: Pick two different maps to see what changed.
results.unavailable: No precomputed diff for this pair. One of the two builds is missing its unfilled (source data) variant.
info: The IPv4/IPv6 toggle by the section title scopes everything below it: the match banner, bucket counts, stacked bar, and top movers. Bitcoin Core treats the families as independent, so 10,000 IPv4 prefixes moving means something different from 10,000 IPv6.

The match banner counts in peer diversity buckets (/16 for IPv4, /32 for IPv6), the buckets Bitcoin Core's GetGroup() uses with no asmap loaded. A bucket counts as changed when any prefix inside it gained, lost, or swapped its ASN between Map A and Map B. The denominator is every bucket either map covers, so the match can never read below zero.
Within the selected family, each entry level change falls into exactly one of three groups.

info.text: A prefix mapped in both maps but now on a different autonomous system. Where most ASmap edits land.
A prefix had no autonomous system in Map A and resolves to one in Map B.
A prefix that resolved in Map A no longer does in Map B.

info: Computed from the unfilled variant of each build (named under the bucket count), so each change is a real BGP, RPKI, or IRR shift, not a fill artefact.

nodeImpact.caption: of observed nodes change AS ({affected} of {total})
nodeImpact.source: {source} crawl ({date}), each node resolved in Map A and Map B.
nodeImpact.unavailable: Real node impact is not available for this pair yet.
info: How many observed Bitcoin nodes resolve to a different autonomous system between the two maps you picked. It turns the prefix diff above into real peers affected, so it changes with the pair.
Each crawled node is looked up in both maps and bucketed like the prefixes above: reassigned, newly mapped, or unmapped. Scoped to the selected family.
The crawl data is non-public, so only aggregate counts are published, never addresses. A pair shows no line until the committed network data covers both its builds.

diff.rosterDelta: ASes mapped: {a} in Map A, {b} in Map B. Appeared {appeared}, disappeared {disappeared}.
rosterDeltaInfo: How many distinct autonomous systems each build maps, and how that roster changed between Map A and Map B.

rosterDeltaInfo.text: Autonomous systems in Map B but not Map A.
Autonomous systems in Map A but not Map B.

rosterDeltaInfo: Unlike the buckets above, this counts ASes across both families, so the toggle does not change it.

topMovers.title: Top Movers
topMovers.empty: No top movers in the selected diff.
topMovers.noMatches: No autonomous systems match this filter.
info: Autonomous systems most affected by the diff, ranked by the address space they gained or lost in the active family.

The IPv4/IPv6 toggle scopes this table too. Each AS appears once per family and can rank very differently: a single large IPv4 prefix barely moves the IPv6 ranking, and dozens of tiny IPv6 blocks barely show under IPv4. That is why the two are not merged.

info.text: This AS's share of the total address space the diff moved in the active family. Hover a row for the raw counts.

Up gained, down lost, a double arrow means space moved both ways. The counterpart can be a real autonomous system or the unmapped pool: up-unmapped gained space that had no ASN, down-unmapped lost space that no longer resolves.

info: Hover any row for the operator name, the raw counts moved, how much address space the operator holds on each side, and Touched — how much of the operator's own footprint this change moved. It can read above 100% when one build stores the operator as a single block and the other splits it into many.

tooltip.exchangedUnmapped: exchanged addresses with the unmapped pool
tooltip.newlyMapped: gained addresses from the unmapped pool (newly mapped)
tooltip.unmapped: addresses no longer resolve to any ASN
